summary: Gruber dissects Elon Musk's public appeal to Tim Cook for a special App Store fee arrangement on X/Twitter creator subscriptions. He argues Apple has no incentive to grant X Corp any exception, especially given Musk's stated ambition to turn X into an 'everything app' that would directly compete with iOS as a platform. Gruber notes the irony that Musk only calls out Apple and not Google, likely because iOS users spend far more money. He also points out that X could simply move subscriptions to the web but doesn't, which implicitly validates Apple's argument that the platform drives purchases.
key_points: Apple has no reason to carve out special App Store terms for X Corp, and certainly not when Musk's goal is to build a competing platform
The 'everything app' vision directly threatens iOS's role as the bedrock platform, making Apple cooperation even less likely
Musk singles out Apple but ignores Google's 15% cut on Android, likely because iOS users spend significantly more
X could bypass App Store fees by moving subscriptions to the web, but doesn't — implicitly proving Apple's case that iOS drives purchases
Gruber argues Apple should move to a flat 85/15 split for non-gaming transactions
money_quotes: Why in the world would they grant such an exception to X Corp, when Musk's oft-stated goal is to grow the platform into an 'everything app' that encompasses chat, shopping, banking, entertainment, publishing, ride-hailing, and more?
We already have platforms that encompass everything: iOS and Android.
This is why Apple (and Google) feel fully justified in taking their cuts of digital-goods transactions on their platforms — they're the ones who built the platforms that enable this commerce.
If Musk were to succeed in building X into an 'everything app', it would implicitly mean usurping the role iOS and Android play today as the everything platforms.
Why would Apple help X Corp get started down that path by agreeing to let them conduct reduced-fee — let alone no-fee — transactions?
I suspect they're escaping Musk's attention simply because people on Android spend so much less money than people on iOS.

People are far less likely to subscribe over the web — which makes Apple's case for them that they're entitled to a hefty cut for having built a platform where people are so willing to buy and subscribe to things.
key_insight: Musk's request for Apple to subsidize X's creator subscriptions is self-defeating because his stated ambition to build an 'everything app' means asking Apple to help build a competitor to its own platform.
